activities.title: Why this distinction matters
activities.content:
Understanding which expenses are **fixed** and which are **variable** is crucial for budgeting because they require different strategies:

- **Fixed expenses** are predictable — you know exactly what you'll pay
- **Variable expenses** fluctuate — harder to predict, easier to overspend

Most people's budget problems come from underestimating variable expenses, especially food and entertainment.
activities.notePrompt: Can you name two of your fixed expenses and two variable ones right now?

activities.title: Fixed expenses
activities.content:
**Fixed expenses** stay the same regardless of your behaviour:

- Rent (same amount every week/month)
- Hire purchase / finance agreements
- Subscription services (Netflix, Spotify, gym)
- Insurance premiums
- Minimum loan repayments
- Phone plan (if on a plan, not prepaid)

**Advantage:** Easy to budget — you know exactly what's coming
**Disadvantage:** Hard to reduce quickly — usually locked in by contracts

Fixed expenses are your floor — the minimum you must earn to survive.
activities.notePrompt: List your fixed expenses. What is the minimum income you need to cover them all?

activities.title: Variable expenses
activities.content:
**Variable expenses** change week to week based on your choices:

- Groceries (varies by what you buy)
- Fuel (varies by how much you drive)
- Dining out and takeaways
- Clothing and shoes
- Entertainment (concerts, movies, events)
- Personal care (haircuts, cosmetics)
- Gifts and celebrations

**Advantage:** You can control these — your biggest lever for saving more
**Disadvantage:** Easy to underestimate and overspend

Most people underestimate variable expenses by 25-35% when asked to guess.
activities.notePrompt: Which variable expense do you think you spend the most on? What is your best guess for the weekly amount?

questions.statement: Grocery spending is a fixed expense because you buy food every week.
questions.explanation: Groceries are variable — the amount changes based on what you buy, where you shop, and how many meals you eat at home. The category is regular, but the amount varies.

questions.statement: Fixed expenses are generally harder to reduce than variable expenses.
questions.explanation: Cancelling a gym contract or phone plan is harder than deciding to cook at home instead of ordering takeaways. Variable expenses give you immediate control.

questions.statement: A Spotify subscription is a variable expense.
questions.explanation: Spotify charges the same amount every month — it is a fixed expense. Even though it is optional, the amount is predictable and does not vary with your behaviour.

questions.question: Lena wants to reduce her expenses by $50/week immediately. Which category should she focus on first?
options: Variable expenses — she can control them daily
It does not matter which one
questions.explanation: Variable expenses can be reduced immediately through daily choices (cooking more, fewer takeaways, skipping one outing). Fixed expenses usually require notice periods or contract penalties to change.

questions.question: Which of the following is a FIXED expense?
questions.explanation: A monthly gym membership is fixed — same amount debited every month regardless of how many times you go. The others all vary based on usage and choices.

activities.title: Semi-variable expenses
activities.content:
Some expenses blur the line — they're **semi-variable**:

- **Power:** Fixed connection fee + variable usage
- **Phone:** Fixed plan + variable data/call charges
- **Car:** Fixed insurance + variable fuel and maintenance
- **Food:** Fixed household items + variable treats and dining

For budgeting purposes, use your highest recent month as the estimate for semi-variable expenses. This builds in a buffer rather than leaving you short.
activities.notePrompt: Identify one semi-variable expense in your life and estimate its typical range (e.g. power: $40-80/month).

activities.instruction: Sort these expenses into Fixed or Variable

activities.title: Reducing fixed costs — the bigger win
activities.content:
While variable expenses are easier to cut daily, reducing fixed costs creates permanent savings:

**High-impact fixed cost reductions:**
- Moving to a cheaper flat or adding a flatmate: save $100-200/week
- Switching phone plans: save $20-50/month
- Cancelling unused subscriptions: save $30-80/month
- Refinancing at a lower interest rate: save hundreds per year
- Moving closer to work (trade longer commute): save $30-100/week in fuel

Each dollar removed from fixed costs saves that amount every single period — no willpower required.
activities.notePrompt: Can you identify one fixed expense you could reduce or eliminate? How much would you save per year?

activities.prompt: James pays $85/week for petrol, $40/month for Spotify Premium, $29.99/month for a gaming subscription, and $49/month for a gym he barely uses. Calculate his monthly fixed subscription costs and suggest which to cut and why.
activities.sampleAnswer: Monthly subscriptions: Spotify $40 + gaming $29.99 + gym $49 = $118.99/month. The gym ($49/month) is the best target — if he rarely uses it he is paying $588/year for nothing. The gaming subscription ($29.99) could be put on pause if he is not actively playing. Petrol is variable and harder to cut unless he changes behaviour or route. Cancelling both gym and gaming saves $78.99/month = $947.88/year.
activities.hint: Add up the monthly subscription costs, then evaluate which has the least value for the money.
